name: Puente Cogolludo from Orellana de la Sierra – round trip through the Parque del Embalse de Orellana and Sierra de Pela
name_original: Puente Cogolludo desde Orellana de la Sierra – ida y vuelta por el Parque del Embalse de Orellana y Sierra de Pela
region_intro: Hiking around Orellana De La Sierra offers diverse landscapes, situated at the foot of the Sierra de Pela and bordering the vast Orellana Reservoir. The terrain features hard quartzite cliffs, red earth slopes, and fertile peneplains, transitioning through Mediterranean scrubland, dehesas, and pine and oak woodlands. The region is part of the Natura 2000 network, designated for its ecological significance and birdlife. Best hiking trails around Orellana De La Sierra The most popular hiking route is Puente Cogolludo from Orellana de la Sierra – round trip through the Parque del Embalse de Orellana and Sierra de Pela, a 12.0 miles (19.3 km) trail that takes 5 hours 15 minutes to complete. This difficult route passes the historic Puente Cogolludo and offers views of the Orellana Reservoir. Another top favourite among local hikers is Orellana la Vieja from Playa de las Casetas – circular by the Embalse and Canal de Orellana, a moderate 10.3 miles (16.5 km) path. This trail explores the surroundings of the Orellana Reservoir and its canal, offering varied scenery. Local hikers also love the Playa de Orellana loop from Orellana la Vieja, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail leading through the area around Playa de Orellana, often completed in about 1 hour 19 minutes. Hiking around Orellana De La Sierra is defined by the Orellana Reservoir, the Sierra de Pela, and diverse habitats including Mediterranean scrubland and dehesas. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to challenging treks. The routes in Orellana De La Sierra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from 5 reviews. More than 20 visitors have used komoot to explore Orellana De La Sierra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Orellana De La Sierra?
A: There are &lt;em&gt;over 8&lt;/em&gt; hiking routes available around Orellana De La Sierra. These trails offer a variety of experiences, from easy strolls to more challenging treks, exploring the diverse landscapes of the region.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Orellana De La Sierra?
A: The optimal time for hiking in Orellana de la Sierra is during &lt;strong&gt;spring and autumn&lt;/strong&gt;. During these seasons, temperatures are pleasant, making it ideal to explore the region's diverse landscapes without the extreme heat of summer or the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Orellana De La Sierra?
A: Yes, Orellana De La Sierra offers several easy hiking options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40015092" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de Orellana loop from Orellana la Vieja&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.1-mile (5.0 km) trail that takes about 1 hour 19 minutes to complete, perfect for a relaxed walk near the reservoir.

Q: Where can I find more challenging hikes around Orellana De La Sierra?
A: For those seeking a challenge, the region provides difficult routes that delve deeper into the landscape. A demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527538" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellana de la Sierra Village – Playa de Orellana loop from Orellana la Vieja&lt;/a&gt;, a 12.9-mile (20.8 km) trail with significant elevation changes, offering a rigorous experience for regular hikers.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I expect to see on hikes in this region?
A: Hiking in Orellana de la Sierra allows you to experience a rich variety of natural features. You'll encounter the vast &lt;strong&gt;Orellana Reservoir&lt;/strong&gt;, often called Extremadura's 'inland sea,' and the mountainous backdrop of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de Pela&lt;/strong&gt;. The trails traverse diverse habitats including Mediterranean scrubland, dehesas (oak meadows), and quartzite cliffs. You might also spot historical elements like the ancient &lt;strong&gt;Puente Cogolludo&lt;/strong&gt; bridge.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Orellana De La Sierra?
A: Yes, there are circular routes available. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1101892674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellana la Vieja from Playa de las Casetas – circular by the Embalse and Canal de Orellana&lt;/a&gt;. This moderate 10.3-mile (16.5 km) path allows you to explore the surroundings of the Orellana Reservoir and its canal in a loop.

Q: What kind of terrain will I encounter on the hiking trails?
A: The terrain around Orellana De La Sierra is quite varied. You'll hike through hard, gray quartzite cliffs, red earth slopes, and fertile peneplains. The routes also pass through Mediterranean scrubland, pastures, jarares (rockrose bushes), dehesas (oak meadows), and shaded pine and oak woodlands, reflecting the region's diverse ecological richness.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Orellana De La Sierra?
A: The hiking routes in Orellana De La Sierra are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, the stunning views of the Orellana Reservoir, and the opportunity to explore both natural beauty and historical sites like the Puente Cogolludo.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest to explore while hiking?
A: Beyond its natural beauty, the region offers several points of historical and cultural interest. You can encounter the ruins of the Roman city of Lacimurga, and in Orellana de la Sierra village itself, explore structures like the &lt;strong&gt;Castle and Palace of the Orellana&lt;/strong&gt;. The historic Puente Cogolludo is also a significant landmark along some routes.

Q: Is Orellana de la Sierra a good destination for birdwatching during a hike?
A: Yes, Orellana de la Sierra is an excellent destination for birdwatching. The area, particularly around the Orellana Reservoir and Sierra de Pela, is part of the Natura 2000 network and designated as a Special Protection Area for Birds (SPA). Hikers often have the chance to spot species like golden eagles, black storks, and griffon vultures.

Q: Are there any trails that offer views of the Orellana Reservoir?
A: Many trails in the region offer spectacular views of the Orellana Reservoir. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1102968568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Torretas from Navalvillar de Pela – round trip via Sierra de Pela&lt;/a&gt; provides scenic vistas as it winds through the Sierra de Pela, overlooking the vast 'inland sea'.

Q: Can I find trails that are part of longer-distance paths?
A: Yes, Orellana de la Sierra is connected to longer-distance routes. The nearby town of Orellana la Vieja is a strategic stop on the &lt;strong&gt;Guadiana Natural Path&lt;/strong&gt;. Stage 24 of this path introduces hikers to the Orellana surroundings, crossing the Cogolludo bridge and passing through Orellana de la Sierra, showcasing the region's landscape transition.
tips: Roman Villa of Lacimurga. These ruins are located in the place known as Cogolludo. It is a very pleasant place, next to the Guadiana river.

It's a historic bridge located over the Guadiana River, near the pre-Roman ruins of Lacimurga.
It's part of the Senda de los Embalses (Reservoirs Path), a popular hiking and nature trail.

This town, popularly known as Orellanita, is connected, according to some legends, with Roman times, specifically with a Roman legion called Aurelia, supposedly based in this place, from which the place name Aureliana would have arisen and, from it, Orellana. In its medieval times it is located in the population privilege granted by Enrique II to Alvar García Bejarano, owner of the castle of the Bejaranos and who would change his title to that of Orellana.
